revision.text: #redirect[[en:Koka_Method]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''估価法'''（こかほう・'''沽価法'''）とは、[[古代]]から[[中世]]にかけての[[日本]]において、[[朝廷]]・[[国衙]]・[[鎌倉幕府]]において、[[市場]]における[[公定価格]]及び物品の換算率を定めた[[法律]]。これに基づく[[価格]]を'''估価'''（こか、'''沽価'''）と呼び、[[租税]]の物納や日本国外との[[貿易]]の価格や交換の基準としても用いられた。
[[大宝律令]]には[[首都|都]]の東西両市における估価をはじめ、貿易や[[口分田]]などの[[公田]]からの納税を代物で納めるときの估価などが規定されている。
しかし、[[延暦]]17年（[[798年]]）の[[太政官符]]によれば、[[令制国|諸国]]が地元特産物を朝廷に納めるために買い上げを行う（[[交易雑物]]）時には、[[和市]]の価（今日で言う[[市場価格]]）で購入するように命じられている。これは、估価と和市価格の乖離に加え、国衙の[[国司]]や[[在庁官人]]が不当に安い価格を估価として地元住民に押し付けた上、必要以上に買い入れてその余剰をもって私服を肥やすという不正が後を絶たなかったからである。だが、[[皇朝十二銭]]の廃絶などで[[貨幣]]流通が衰退する一方で代納品による租税の物納やそれに伴う交易による中央への上供品の購入が一般化すると、交換の価値基準としての估価が求められるようになった。また、中央においては都（[[平安京]]）における価格安定や[[大宰府]]における対外貿易の統制（輸入品の多くは平安京で消費されたために、両者には連動性があった）の估価が必要とされていた。
こうした事から以後も估価法は継続され、[[延喜]]14年（[[914年]]）には地方国衙の估価を全国一律（[[絹]]1[[疋]]＝[[稲]]50束、[[綿]]1[[屯]]＝稲5束）を[[国例]]に合わせて変更する事を許し、[[天暦]]元年（[[947年]]）には[[畿内]]と[[丹波国]]に実情に合わせた引き下げを命じている。その後も[[応和]]2年（[[962年]]）・[[寛和]]2年（[[986年]]）・[[延久]]4年（[[1072年]]）・[[治承]]3年（[[1179年]]）に估価法による価格統制が行われ、特に治承3年のものは[[平氏政権]]の主財源であった[[宋銭]]の使用禁止が[[後白河法皇]]の意向で提議された事から同年の[[平清盛]]による[[治承のクーデター]]の原因の一つになったとされている。その後も[[建久]]6年（[[1195年]]）・[[建長]]元年（[[1249年]]）同2年（[[1250年]]）・同5年（[[1253年]]）・[[弘安]]5年（[[1253年]]）・[[元徳]]2年（[[1330年]]）にも出され、鎌倉幕府も建長5年の估価法に呼応して同年と翌年に価格の公定を行っている。

[[Category:経済史|こかほう]] [[Category:通貨制度|こかほう]]

'''估価法'''（こかほう・'''沽価法'''）とは、古代から中世にかけての日本において、朝廷・国衙・鎌倉幕府において、[[市場]]における[[公定価格]]及び物品の換算率を定めた法律。これに基づく[[価格]]を'''估価'''（こか、'''沽価'''）と呼び、租税の物納や日本国外との貿易の価格や交換の基準としても用いられた。

'''估価法'''（こかほう・'''沽価法'''）とは、古代から中世にかけての日本において、朝廷・国衙・鎌倉幕府において、[[市場]]における[[公定価格]]及び物品の換算率を定めた法律。これに基づく[[価格]]を'''估価'''（こか、'''沽価'''）と呼び、租税の物納や日本国外との貿易の価格や交換の基準としても用いられた。
[[大宝律令]]には都の東西両市における估価をはじめ、貿易や口分田などの[[公田]]からの納税（[[地子]]）を代物で納める（あるいは、地子を中央へ送るために[[地子交易]]を行う）ときの估価などが規定されている。
しかし、延暦17年（798年）の太政官符によれば、[[令制国|諸国]]が地元特産物を朝廷に納めるために買い上げを行う（[[交易雑物]]）時には、[[和市]]の価（今日で言う[[市場価格]]）で購入するように命じられている。これは、估価と和市価格の乖離に加え、国衙の国司や在庁官人が不当に安い価格を估価として地元住民に押し付けた上、必要以上に買い入れてその余剰をもって私服を肥やすという不正が後を絶たなかったからである。だが、皇朝十二銭の廃絶などで貨幣流通が衰退する一方で代納品による租税の物納やそれに伴う交易による中央への上供品の購入が一般化すると、交換の価値基準としての估価が求められるようになった。また、中央においては都（平安京）における価格安定や大宰府における対外貿易の統制（輸入品の多くは平安京で消費されたために、両者には連動性があった）の估価が必要とされていた。
こうした事から以後も估価法は継続され、[[延喜]]14年（[[914年]]）には全国一律であった地方国衙の估価（絹1疋＝稲50束、綿1屯＝稲5束）を[[国例]]に合わせて変更する事を許し、[[天暦]]元年（[[947年]]）には畿内と[[丹波国]]に実情に合わせた引き下げを命じている。その後も[[応和]]2年（[[962年]]）・[[寛和]]2年（[[986年]]）・[[延久]]4年（[[1072年]]）・[[治承]]3年（[[1179年]]）に估価法による[[価格統制]]が行われ、特に治承3年のものは平氏政権の主財源であった宋銭の使用禁止が[[後白河法皇]]の意向で提議された事から同年の平清盛による[[治承のクーデター]]の原因の一つになったとされている。その後も[[建久]]6年（[[1195年]]）・[[建長]]元年（[[1249年]]）同2年（[[1250年]]）・同5年（[[1253年]]）・[[弘安]]5年（[[1253年]]）・[[元徳]]2年（[[1330年]]）にも出され、鎌倉幕府も建長5年の估価法に呼応して同年と翌年に価格の公定を行っている。
[[Category:経済史|こかほう]] [[Category:日本の経済史|こかほう]] [[Category:通貨制度|こかほう]] [[Category:平安時代|こかほう]] [[Category:鎌倉時代|こかほう]] [[Category:料金システム|こかほう]]

'''估価法'''（こかほう・'''沽価法'''）とは、古代から中世にかけての日本において、[[朝廷]]・[[国衙]]・[[鎌倉幕府]]において、[[市場]]における[[公定価格]]及び物品の換算率を定めた法律。これに基づく[[価格]]を'''估価'''（こか、'''沽価'''）と呼び、租税の物納や日本国外との貿易の価格や交換の基準としても用いられた。
[[大宝律令]]には都の東西両市における估価をはじめ、貿易や口分田などの[[公田]]からの納税（[[地子]]）を代物で納める（あるいは、地子を中央へ送るために[[地子交易]]を行う）ときの估価などが規定されている。
しかし、延暦17年（[[798年]]）の太政官符によれば、[[令制国|諸国]]が地元特産物を[[朝廷]]に納めるために買い上げを行う（[[交易雑物]]）時には、[[和市]]の価（今日で言う[[市場価格]]）で購入するように命じられている。これは、估価と和市価格の乖離に加え、[[国衙]]の[[国司]]や[[在庁官人]]が不当に安い価格を估価として地元住民に押し付けた上、必要以上に買い入れてその余剰をもって私腹を肥やすという不正が後を絶たなかったからである。だが、皇朝十二銭の廃絶などで貨幣流通が衰退する一方で代納品による租税の物納やそれに伴う交易による中央への上供品の購入が一般化すると、交換の価値基準としての估価が求められるようになった。また、中央においては都（[[平安京]]）における価格安定や[[大宰府]]における対外貿易の統制（輸入品の多くは平安京で消費されたために、両者には連動性があった）の估価が必要とされていた。
こうした事から以後も估価法は継続され、[[延喜]]14年（[[914年]]）には全国一律であった地方国衙の估価（絹1疋＝稲50束、綿1屯＝稲5束）を[[国例]]に合わせて変更する事を許し、[[天暦]]元年（[[947年]]）には畿内と[[丹波国]]に実情に合わせた引き下げを命じている。その後も[[応和]]2年（[[962年]]）・[[寛和]]2年（[[986年]]）・[[延久]]4年（[[1072年]]）・[[治承]]3年（[[1179年]]）に估価法による[[価格統制]]が行われ、特に治承3年のものは[[平氏政権]]の主財源であった[[宋銭]]の使用禁止が[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]の意向で提議された事から同年の[[平清盛]]による[[治承三年の政変]]の原因の一つになったとされている。その後も[[建久]]6年（[[1195年]]）・[[建長]]元年（[[1249年]]）同2年（[[1250年]]）・同5年（[[1253年]]）・[[弘安]]5年（[[1282年]]）・[[元徳]]2年（[[1330年]]）にも出され、[[鎌倉幕府]]も建長5年の估価法に呼応して同年と翌年に価格の公定を行っている。
{{DEFAULTSORT:こかほう}} [[Category:日本の経済史]] [[Category:通貨制度]] [[Category:料金システム]] [[Category:平安時代]] [[Category:鎌倉幕府]]

'''估価法'''（こかほう・'''沽価法'''）とは、古代から中世にかけての日本において、[[朝廷]]・[[国衙]]・[[鎌倉幕府]]において、[[市場]]における[[公定価格]]及び物品の換算率を定めた法律。これに基づく[[価格]]を'''估価'''（こか、'''沽価'''）と呼び、租税の物納や日本国外との貿易の価格や交換の基準としても用いられた。
== 概要 == [[大宝律令]]には都の東西両市における估価をはじめ、貿易や口分田などの[[公田]]からの納税（[[地子]]）を代物で納める（あるいは、地子を中央へ送るために[[地子交易]]を行う）ときの估価などが規定されている。
しかし、延暦17年（[[798年]]）の太政官符によれば、[[令制国|諸国]]が地元特産物を[[朝廷]]に納めるために買い上げを行う（[[交易雑物]]）時には、[[和市]]の価（今日で言う[[市場価格]]）で購入するように命じられている。これは、估価と和市価格の乖離に加え、[[国衙]]の[[国司]]や[[在庁官人]]が不当に安い価格を估価として地元住民に押し付けた上、必要以上に買い入れてその余剰をもって私腹を肥やすという不正が後を絶たなかったからである（ただし、官設の市場と国衙内に[[市司]]の職制を持たない諸国では估価は実施されておらず、実際に買い上げの基準とされたのは都での估価を参考として朝廷が指示した価格である、とする説がある。同時に国衙に市司の制度がないために諸国の市場を管理していたのは[[郡司]]を含めた現地の豪族たちで、実際の買い上げやその価格の決定は彼らが行っていたとされている<ref>宮川麻紀「八世紀における諸国の交易価格と估価」（初出:『日本歴史』778号（2013年）/所収:宮川『日本古代の交易と社会』（吉川弘文館、2020年） ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9） 2020年、P162-184.</ref>）。
その後、皇朝十二銭の廃絶などで貨幣流通が衰退する一方で代納品による租税の物納やそれに伴う交易による中央への上供品の購入が一般化すると、交換の価値基準としての估価が求められるようになった。また、中央においては都（[[平安京]]）における価格安定や[[大宰府]]における対外貿易の統制（輸入品の多くは平安京で消費されたために、両者には連動性があった）の估価が必要とされていた。
こうした事から以後も估価法は継続され、[[延喜]]14年（[[914年]]）には全国一律であった地方国衙の估価（絹1疋＝稲50束、綿1屯＝稲5束）を[[国例]]に合わせて変更する事を許し、[[天暦]]元年（[[947年]]）には畿内と[[丹波国]]に実情に合わせた引き下げを命じている。
その後も[[応和]]2年（[[962年]]）・[[寛和]]2年（[[986年]]）・[[延久]]4年（[[1072年]]）・[[治承]]3年（[[1179年]]）に估価法による[[価格統制]]が行われ、特に治承3年のものは[[平氏政権]]の主財源であった[[宋銭]]の使用禁止が[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]の意向で提議された事から同年の[[平清盛]]による[[治承三年の政変]]の原因の一つになったとされている。
後の鎌倉時代の[[建久]]6年（[[1195年]]）・[[建長]]元年（[[1249年]]）同2年（[[1250年]]）・同5年（[[1253年]]）・[[弘安]]5年（[[1282年]]）・[[元徳]]2年（[[1330年]]）にも出され、[[鎌倉幕府]]も建長5年の估価法に呼応して同年と翌年に価格の公定を行っている。
== 関連項目 == *[[禄物価法]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:こかほう}} [[Category:日本の経済史]] [[Category:料金システム]] [[Category:平安時代の政治]] [[Category:鎌倉幕府法]] [[Category:日本の律令制]]

revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only applied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

== Related Articles == *{{ill|Rokubutsukahō|ja|禄物価法}}

'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only applied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only applied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing pages to myself
revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only applied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] to [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only applied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only applied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only applied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == *{{ill|Rokubutsukahō|ja|禄物価法}}

Some theories suggest that koka were only applied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.

The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only applied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>

The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.

For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only applied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only aqqlied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == *{{ill|Rokubutsukahō|ja|禄物価法}}

revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == *{{ill|Rokubutsukahō|ja|禄物価法|12=draft}}

Text replacement - "|12=draft" to ""
23 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:估価法 up to rev 97910745
revision.text:
'''估価法'''（こかほう・'''沽価法'''）とは、古代から中世にかけての日本において、[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]・[[国衙]]・[[鎌倉幕府]]において、[[市場]]における[[公定価格]]及び物品の換算率を定めた法律。これに基づく[[価格]]を'''估価'''（こか、'''沽価'''）と呼び、租税の物納や日本国外との貿易の価格や交換の基準としても用いられた。
== 概要 == [[大宝律令]]には都の東西両市における估価をはじめ、貿易や口分田などの[[公田]]からの納税（[[地子]]）を代物で納める（あるいは、地子を中央へ送るために[[地子交易]]を行う）ときの估価などが規定されている。
しかし、延暦17年（[[798年]]）の太政官符によれば、[[令制国|諸国]]が地元特産物を[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]に納めるために買い上げを行う（[[交易雑物]]）時には、[[市場#日本|和市]]の価（今日で言う[[市場価格]]）で購入するように命じられている。これは、估価と和市価格の乖離に加え、[[国衙]]の[[国司]]や[[在庁官人]]が不当に安い価格を估価として地元住民に押し付けた上、必要以上に買い入れてその余剰をもって私腹を肥やすという不正が後を絶たなかったからである（ただし、官設の市場と国衙内に[[市司]]の職制を持たない諸国では估価は実施されておらず、実際に買い上げの基準とされたのは都での估価を参考として朝廷が指示した価格である、とする説がある。同時に国衙に市司の制度がないために諸国の市場を管理していたのは[[郡司]]を含めた現地の豪族たちで、実際の買い上げやその価格の決定は彼らが行っていたとされている<ref>宮川麻紀「八世紀における諸国の交易価格と估価」（初出:『日本歴史』778号（2013年）/所収:宮川『日本古代の交易と社会』（吉川弘文館、2020年） ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9） 2020年、P162-184.</ref>）。
その後、皇朝十二銭の廃絶などで貨幣流通が衰退する一方で代納品による租税の物納やそれに伴う交易による中央への上供品の購入が一般化すると、交換の価値基準としての估価が求められるようになった。また、中央においては都（[[平安京]]）における価格安定や[[大宰府]]における対外貿易の統制（輸入品の多くは平安京で消費されたために、両者には連動性があった）の估価が必要とされていた。
こうした事から以後も估価法は継続され、[[延喜]]14年（[[914年]]）には全国一律であった地方国衙の估価（絹1疋＝稲50束、綿1屯＝稲5束）を[[国例]]に合わせて変更する事を許し、[[天暦]]元年（[[947年]]）には畿内と[[丹波国]]に実情に合わせた引き下げを命じている。
その後も[[応和]]2年（[[962年]]）・[[寛和]]2年（[[986年]]）・[[延久]]4年（[[1072年]]）・[[治承]]3年（[[1179年]]）に估価法による[[価格統制]]が行われ、特に治承3年のものは[[平氏政権]]の主財源であった[[宋銭]]の使用禁止が[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]の意向で提議された事から同年の[[平清盛]]による[[治承三年の政変]]の原因の一つになったとされている。
後の鎌倉時代の[[建久]]6年（[[1195年]]）・[[建長]]元年（[[1249年]]）同2年（[[1250年]]）・同5年（[[1253年]]）・[[弘安]]5年（[[1282年]]）・[[元徳]]2年（[[1330年]]）にも出され、[[鎌倉幕府]]も建長5年の估価法に呼応して同年と翌年に価格の公定を行っている。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[禄物価法]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:こかほう}} [[Category:日本の経済史]] [[Category:料金システム]] [[Category:平安時代の政治]] [[Category:鎌倉幕府法]] [[Category:日本の律令制]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[估価法]] to [[Koka Method]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

== 概要 == [[大宝律令]]には都の東西両市における估価をはじめ、貿易や口分田などの[[公田]]からの納税（[[地子]]）を代物で納める（あるいは、地子を中央へ送るために[[地子交易]]を行う）ときの估価などが規定されている。
しかし、延暦17年（[[798年]]）の太政官符によれば、[[令制国|諸国]]が地元特産物を[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]に納めるために買い上げを行う（[[交易雑物]]）時には、[[市場#日本|和市]]の価（今日で言う[[市場価格]]）で購入するように命じられている。これは、估価と和市価格の乖離に加え、[[国衙]]の[[国司]]や[[在庁官人]]が不当に安い価格を估価として地元住民に押し付けた上、必要以上に買い入れてその余剰をもって私腹を肥やすという不正が後を絶たなかったからである（ただし、官設の市場と国衙内に[[市司]]の職制を持たない諸国では估価は実施されておらず、実際に買い上げの基準とされたのは都での估価を参考として朝廷が指示した価格である、とする説がある。同時に国衙に市司の制度がないために諸国の市場を管理していたのは[[郡司]]を含めた現地の豪族たちで、実際の買い上げやその価格の決定は彼らが行っていたとされている<ref>宮川麻紀「八世紀における諸国の交易価格と估価」（初出:『日本歴史』778号（2013年）/所収:宮川『日本古代の交易と社会』（吉川弘文館、2020年） ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9） 2020年、P162-184.</ref>）。
その後、皇朝十二銭の廃絶などで貨幣流通が衰退する一方で代納品による租税の物納やそれに伴う交易による中央への上供品の購入が一般化すると、交換の価値基準としての估価が求められるようになった。また、中央においては都（[[平安京]]）における価格安定や[[大宰府]]における対外貿易の統制（輸入品の多くは平安京で消費されたために、両者には連動性があった）の估価が必要とされていた。
こうした事から以後も估価法は継続され、[[延喜]]14年（[[914年]]）には全国一律であった地方国衙の估価（絹1疋＝稲50束、綿1屯＝稲5束）を[[国例]]に合わせて変更する事を許し、[[天暦]]元年（[[947年]]）には畿内と[[丹波国]]に実情に合わせた引き下げを命じている。
その後も[[応和]]2年（[[962年]]）・[[寛和]]2年（[[986年]]）・[[延久]]4年（[[1072年]]）・[[治承]]3年（[[1179年]]）に估価法による[[価格統制]]が行われ、特に治承3年のものは[[平氏政権]]の主財源であった[[宋銭]]の使用禁止が[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]の意向で提議された事から同年の[[平清盛]]による[[治承三年の政変]]の原因の一つになったとされている。
後の鎌倉時代の[[建久]]6年（[[1195年]]）・[[建長]]元年（[[1249年]]）同2年（[[1250年]]）・同5年（[[1253年]]）・[[弘安]]5年（[[1282年]]）・[[元徳]]2年（[[1330年]]）にも出され、[[鎌倉幕府]]も建長5年の估価法に呼応して同年と翌年に価格の公定を行っている。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[禄物価法]]

revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == *{{ill|Rokubutsukahō|ja|禄物価法|12=draft}}

This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[Imperial Court of Japan|Imperial Court]], [[kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[:en:Kanna|Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[:en:Jishō|Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[Kōan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.

During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[:en:Koan|Koan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.
