revision.comment: Merge [[Category:日本の律令制]] into [[Category:Ritsuryō System of Japan]] via bot
revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[:en:Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]], [[:en:Kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[:en:Kanna|Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[:en:Jishō|Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[:en:Koan|Koan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

revision.text:
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[:en:Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]], [[:en:Kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[:en:market price|market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[:en:Kanna|Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[:en:Jishō|Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[:en:Song dynasty coins|Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[:en:Koan|Koan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == *{{ill|Rokubutsukahō|ja|禄物価法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokubutsukahō|qq=}}

During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[:en:Koan|Koan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

<!--Koka Method--><!--Koka Method--> [[Category:Politics of the Heian Period]] [[Category:Pricing]] [[Category:Ritsuryō System of Japan]] [[Category:Kamakura Shogunate Law]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11381504}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[d:" to "[[da:d:"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381504]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381504]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[:en:Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]], [[:en:Kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[:en:market price|market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[:en:Kanna|Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[:en:Jishō|Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[:en:Song dynasty coins|Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[:en:Koan|Koan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381504]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381504]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[:en:Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]], [[:en:Kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[:en:market price|market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[:en:Kanna|Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[:en:Jishō|Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[:en:Song dynasty coins|Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[:en:Koan|Koan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

The [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[:en:market price|market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[:en:Kanna|Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[:en:Jishō|Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[:en:Song dynasty coins|Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[:en:Koan|Koan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[:en:market price|market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[:en:Kanna|Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[:en:Jishō|Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[:en:Song dynasty coins|Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[:en:Koan|Koan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[:en:Kanna|Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[:en:Jishō|Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[:en:Song dynasty coins|Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[:en:Koan|Koan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381504]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381504]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the [[:en:Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]], [[:en:Kokuga|provincial governments]], and the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's [[:en:market price|market price]]) when delivering goods to the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]]. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] market stability and foreign trade management at the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of [[:en:Engi|Engi]] (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]].
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in [[:en:Ōwa|Ōwa]] 2 (962), [[:en:Kanna|Kanna]] 2 (986), [[:en:Enkyū|Enkyū]] 4 (1072), and [[:en:Jishō|Jishō]] 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[:en:Song dynasty coins|Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]'s court.
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], further koka regulations were issued in [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū]] 6 (1195), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 1 (1249), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 2 (1250), [[:en:Kenchō|Kenchō]] 5 (1253), [[:en:Koan|Koan]] 5 (1282), and [[:en:Genkō|Genkō]] 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

The capital's {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} market stability and foreign trade management at the {{ill|Dazaifu|en|Dazaifu|WD=Q1179277|lt=Dazaifu}} further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of {{ill|Engi|en|Engi|WD=Q1030330|lt=Engi}} (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In {{ill|Tenryaku|en|Tenryaku|WD=Q1346281|lt=Tenryaku}} 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}}.
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in {{ill|Ōwa|en|Ōwa|WD=Q1324768|lt=Ōwa}} 2 (962), {{ill|Kanna|en|Kanna|WD=Q409365|lt=Kanna}} 2 (986), {{ill|Enkyū|en|Enkyū|WD=Q1193638|lt=Enkyū}} 4 (1072), and {{ill|Jishō|en|Jishō|WD=Q1194671|lt=Jishō}} 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[:en:Song dynasty coins|Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}'s court.
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, further koka regulations were issued in {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū}} 6 (1195), {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō}} 1 (1249), {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō}} 2 (1250), {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō}} 5 (1253), {{ill|Koan|en|Koan|WD=Q1231764|lt=Koan}} 5 (1282), and {{ill|Genkō|en|Genkō|WD=Q844859|lt=Genkō}} 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]]
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|WD=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court}}, {{ill|Kokuga|en|Kokuga|WD=Q11418465|lt=provincial governments}}, and the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku}} (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's {{ill|market price|en|market price|WD=Q180107|lt=market price}}) when delivering goods to the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court}}. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|WD=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi}} (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} market stability and foreign trade management at the {{ill|Dazaifu|en|Dazaifu|WD=Q1179277|lt=Dazaifu}} further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of {{ill|Engi|en|Engi|WD=Q1030330|lt=Engi}} (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In {{ill|Tenryaku|en|Tenryaku|WD=Q1346281|lt=Tenryaku}} 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}}.
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in {{ill|Ōwa|en|Ōwa|WD=Q1324768|lt=Ōwa}} 2 (962), {{ill|Kanna|en|Kanna|WD=Q409365|lt=Kanna}} 2 (986), {{ill|Enkyū|en|Enkyū|WD=Q1193638|lt=Enkyū}} 4 (1072), and {{ill|Jishō|en|Jishō|WD=Q1194671|lt=Jishō}} 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[:en:Song dynasty coins|Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}'s court.
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, further koka regulations were issued in {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū}} 6 (1195), {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō}} 1 (1249), {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō}} 2 (1250), {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō}} 5 (1253), {{ill|Koan|en|Koan|WD=Q1231764|lt=Koan}} 5 (1282), and {{ill|Genkō|en|Genkō|WD=Q844859|lt=Genkō}} 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == *{{ill|Rokubutsukahō|ja|禄物価法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokubutsukahō|1=rokumotsukahou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591169}}
{{translated page|ja|估価法|version=97910745|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:估価法]]
'''Kokahō''' (估価法・沽価法) refers to a law in ancient to medieval Japan that regulated [[fixed market prices]] and conversion rates for goods under the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|WD=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court in Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kokuga|en|Kokuga|WD=Q11418465|lt=provincial governments|1=Kokuga}}, and the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}. Prices established under this system were called '''koka''' (估価・沽価) and were used as standards for taxes paid in kind and trade prices, including those in foreign trade.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} stipulated the koka for the Eastern and Western Markets of the capital and specified conversion rates for taxation from [[kōden]] (public lands) and for bartering goods as tax payments (or exchanging taxes via [[land-tax trading]]).
However, according to a directive issued in the 17th year of {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} (798), provincial governments were instructed to purchase local specialty products at market prices (equivalent to today's {{ill|market price|en|market price|WD=Q180107|lt=market price|1=Market price}}) when delivering goods to the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court}}. This was due to discrepancies between the koka and market prices, as well as abuses by {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|WD=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi}} (provincial governors) and [[zaichō kanjin]] (provincial officials), who imposed unreasonably low koka on local residents and enriched themselves through surplus goods.
Some theories suggest that koka were only a11lied in centrally managed markets and that the Imperial Court prescribed prices for provinces lacking market authorities, relying on local [[gōzoku|land-owning elites]] for actual purchasing decisions and price setting.<ref>Miyakawa, Maki. "Prices and Koka in the Provinces of the Eighth Century," in *Japan History* Issue 778 (2013), reprinted in *Ancient Japan's Trade and Society* (Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2020), ISBN 978-4-642-04658-9.</ref>
With the decline of coin circulation following the cessation of [[Kōchō Jūnisen]], payment of taxes in goods and bartering for tribute to the capital became common. As a result, the koka system was maintained as a standard of value for exchanges. The capital's {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} market stability and foreign trade management at the {{ill|Dazaifu|en|Dazaifu|WD=Q1179277|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu}} further necessitated koka-based regulations.
Subsequent revisions to the koka system included adaptations to local conditions. For example, in the 14th year of {{ill|Engi|en|Engi|WD=Q1030330|lt=Engi|1=Engi}} (914), provincial kokuga were allowed to adjust koka (e.g., silk 1 hiki = 50 sheaves of rice, cotton 1 ton = 5 sheaves of rice) according to [[koku-ryō]] (provincial examples). In {{ill|Tenryaku|en|Tenryaku|WD=Q1346281|lt=Tenryaku|1=Tenryaku}} 1 (947), adjustments were mandated for areas like the Kinai and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}}.
Notable enforcement of koka laws occurred in {{ill|Ōwa|en|Ōwa|WD=Q1324768|lt=Ōwa|1=Ōwa}} 2 (962), {{ill|Kanna|en|Kanna|WD=Q409365|lt=Kanna|1=Kanna}} 2 (986), {{ill|Enkyū|en|Enkyū|WD=Q1193638|lt=Enkyū|1=Enkyū}} 4 (1072), and {{ill|Jishō|en|Jishō|WD=Q1194671|lt=Jishō|1=Jishō}} 3 (1179). The latter case, tied to prohibitions on [[:en:Song dynasty coins|Song dynasty coins]] (a key resource of the [[Taira clan]] government), was a contributing factor to the [[Jishō San-nen no Seihen|Jishō political upheaval]] led by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] against {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}'s court.
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, further koka regulations were issued in {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū|1=Kenkyū}} 6 (1195), {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō|1=Kenchō}} 1 (1249), {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō|1=Kenchō}} 2 (1250), {{ill|Kenchō|en|Kenchō|WD=Q1194270|lt=Kenchō|1=Kenchō}} 5 (1253), {{ill|Koan|en|Koan|WD=Q1231764|lt=Koan|1=Koan}} 5 (1282), and {{ill|Genkō|en|Genkō|WD=Q844859|lt=Genkō|1=Genkō}} 2 (1330). The Kamakura shogunate implemented official price controls in 1253 and 1254 in response to the 1253 koka laws.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == *{{ill|Rokubutsukahō|ja|禄物価法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokubutsukahō|1=rokumotsukahou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591169}}
{{translated page|ja|估価法|version=97910745|comment=Imported full ja history}}
